title: Feathering filler
subtitle: Taper the edges so the patch disappears under paint
excerpt: The core filler-finishing technique: drawing the filling knife outward from the filled area in progressively wider, lighter strokes so the filler tapers to nothing at the edge and becomes invisible once painted.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering and Decorating syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s).
aliases: how to feather filler on a wall

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of drawing a filling or taping knife outward from a filled hole, leaving the filler thicker in the middle and tapering to zero at the edges. The feather hides the patch under the paint coat.

content.text: Most filler failures are not filler failures. The filler is in the hole and the surface has been sanded flat, but the patch still shows under the paint as a cloudy ring or a subtle ridge. The cause is almost always the edge: where the filler meets the surrounding plaster, the transition is abrupt rather than gradual. The technique of
solves this. A feathered patch has no visible boundary.
For pressing filler into the hole.
The wider blade is the feathering tool. A 150 mm knife gives even gentler transitions on large patches.
Ready-mixed is workable for longer than powder filler. Pink-to-white colour change indicates full cure.

Press filler into the hole with the 50 mm knife using diagonal strokes at 45 degrees, packing filler into the back of the hole before drawing the knife across the surface. The filler should stand slightly proud of the wall, not sunken below it.

Draw the 50 mm knife flat along the wall to bring the surface approximately level. Do not press hard enough to pull filler back out of the hole. The surface should be within 1 mm of the surrounding plaster.

Switch to the 100 mm knife. Hold it at about 10 to 15 degrees to the wall, nearly flat. Start each stroke in the centre of the patch and draw outward toward the edge of the filler, pressing firmly at the start and releasing pressure as the knife approaches the surrounding plaster. The blade flexes as the pressure reduces, lifting away from the surface near the edge. This is what creates the taper. Work outward from the centre in all four directions.

The feathering zone should extend at least 50 mm beyond the filled area on each side. Larger patches need wider feathering: a hole 60 mm across needs feathering out to at least 120 mm from the centre of the hole on each side. The broader the feather, the gentler the transition, and the more invisible the finished patch.

Ready-mixed filler is fully cured when it is uniformly white and cool to the touch. In a warm room, allow at least 90 minutes. Sanding wet filler smears it, destroys the feather edge, and clogs the sandpaper.

Sand the cured patch with 120-grit on the sanding block, using circular strokes. The goal is to remove any filler that stands proud of the plaster and to further blend the edge. Finish with 180-grit for a smoother base for the mist coat. After sanding, run a fingertip across the patch boundary: it should be imperceptible.
What a correct result looks like

Hold a lamp at a low angle (raking light) across the repaired surface. A correctly feathered patch casts no visible shadow. The boundary where filler meets plaster should not catch the light. If a ring or shadow is visible, extend the feathering zone further and re-sand.

If the knife lifts vertically at the end of the stroke, it leaves a small ridge at the point of lift. Keep the wrist dropping as the hand moves outward, lowering the knife angle progressively to near-parallel with the wall.
A feathered zone narrower than the hole itself merely moves the abrupt edge outward by a few millimetres. The zone must be at least twice the diameter of the hole on each side.
Uniform pressure takes the same amount of filler all the way to the edge and leaves a shoulder. The taper comes from releasing pressure, not from pressing lightly throughout.

Even a perfectly feathered patch will show as a different sheen under full-strength paint because bare filler and bare plaster absorb paint at different rates. Always apply a diluted mist coat (10 percent water to emulsion) over the filled area before the finish coat.
